titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Celia Shapiro Papers
titlestmt.author: compiled by Elizabeth Pauk

controlnote.p: This encoded finding aid is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0.

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Friday November 13, 2020 at 10:19

descriptivenote.p: Most of the papers are in Russian, with some Yiddish- and English-language material as well.

did.abstract: The papers include the correspondence, writings, and memoirs of Celia Kamenetzky Shapiro and her husband, Boris Shapiro. Most of the papers are in Russian, with some Yiddish- and English-language material as well. Correspondence includes letters and postcards, primarily from the 1930s, to Celia and Boris Shapiro from friends and colleagues in Prague and Paris. Celia Shapiro's diaries and memoirs describe her journey to England, Germany, and the Soviet Union, with interesting observations on Jewish life in Germany in 1936. Writings about Celia Shapiro include book manuscripts and the memoirs of Boris Shapiro. There are also issues of various Soviet emigre publications, and photographs of the Shapiros and their associates. Celia Kamenetzky Shapiro, also known as Tsilia Savel'evna Shapiro and Tsili Kamenetskaia-Shapiro, was a young Russian, Jewish revolutionary associated with the populist Socialist Revolutionary party in the early years of the twentieth century. She emigrated to the United States prior to World War I. Shapiro returned to the USSR for a visit in 1936, writing about her trip in the New York Yiddish-language newspaper The Jewish Daily Forward .

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Copyright has been transferred to Yale University for unpublished materials authored or otherwise produced by the creator(s) of this collection. Copyright status for other collection materials is unknown. Transmission or reproduction of materials protected by U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) beyond that allowed by fair use requires the written permission of the copyright owners. Works not in the public domain cannot be commercially exploited without permission of the copyright owners. Responsibility for any use rests exclusively with the user.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Celia Shapiro Papers (MS 1684). Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library.

item.ref: III. Writings about Celia Shapiro, 1904-1967, n.d.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Celia Kamenetzky Shapiro, also known as Tsilia Savel'evna Shapiro and Tsili Kamenetskaia-Shapiro, was a Russian-Jewish revolutionary associated with the populist Social Revolutionary party in the early years of the twentieth century. She emigrated to the United States prior to World War I. Shapiro returned to the Soviet Union for a visit in 1936, writing about her trip in the New York Yiddish-language newspaper, The Jewish Daily Forward.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The papers include the correspondence, writings, and memoirs of Celia Kamenetzky Shapiro and her husband, Boris Shapiro. Most of the papers are in Russian, with some Yiddish- and English-language material as well. Correspondence includes letters and postcards, primarily from the 1930s, to Celia and Boris Shapiro from friends and colleagues in Prague and Paris. Celia Shapiro's diaries and memoirs describe her journey to England, Germany, and the Soviet Union, with interesting observations on Jewish life in Germany in 1936. Writings about Celia Shapiro include book manuscripts and the memoirs of Boris Shapiro. There are also issues of various Soviet emigre publications, and photographs of the Shapiros and their associates. Celia Kamenetzky Shapiro, also known as Tsilia Savel'evna Shapiro and Tsili Kamenetskaia-Shapiro, was a young Russian, Jewish revolutionary associated with the populist Socialist Revolutionary party in the early years of the twentieth century. She emigrated to the United States prior to World War I. Shapiro returned to the USSR for a visit in 1936, writing about her trip in the New York Yiddish-language newspaper, .

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The correspondence series is arranged chronologically and consists chiefly of letters and postcards to Celia and Boris Shapiro from friends and colleagues, mostly sent from Prague and Paris. The bulk of the correspondence is from the 1930s and is written in Russian. Items of interest include a 1937 letter from V. Zenzinov discussing Russian emigres and party activities in Paris; letters in 1938 from V. Archangelsky regarding Russian emigres in Prague and mentioning prominent emigre Egor Egorovich Lazarev; 1906 letters from an official of Tobolskaya Gubernia who helped Shapiro to escape from Russia; a 1907 letter from the wife of Lenin's younger brother, Dmitry Ulianovych; and a 1941 report of the status of the Russian Socialist Revolutionary Group in New York. There is also some correspondence found in Series II and III.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The writings by Celia Shapiro consist of her diaries and memoirs, written in a mixture of Russian and English. The travel diary describes her journey in 1936 to England, Germany, and the Soviet Union, with interesting observations on Jewish life in Germany. The memoirs in letter form also make references to that trip and to other events in her past.

did.unittitle: Travel diary [entries in Russian and English]
Letters [two in Russian and one in English, making references to her life in Russia]
Notebook [possibly containing notes from a conversation or interview]
Diary [chiefly in Russian with a few entries in English, mostly concerning Shapiro's everyday life and philosophical observations]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series consists of writings and other material about Celia Shapiro, and is divided into three sections: book manuscripts, memoirs of Boris Shapiro, and other material. The book manuscripts consist of typed and written drafts with photographs, letters, and clippings inserted into the text. The material originally contained in Binder #1 appears to be part of the written manuscript; however, it has been kept separately in its original page order. Photographs in Binder #2 have been removed to the photograph series.

did.unittitle: Manuscript for a book about Celia Shapiro [untitled]
Typed drafts [in Russian; pages numbered throughout but occasionally out of order; author unknown]
Typed drafts [in Russian; pages numbered throughout but occasionally out of order; author unknown]
Manuscript for a book about Celia Shapiro [untitled; written drafts in Russian; numbered as pp. 1-2, 107-230; author unknown]
Archangelsky, Vasiliy reminiscences with introduction by Boris Shapiro [typescripts in Russian]
"Binder #1" [written in Russian with occasional English passages; numbered as pp. 87-99, 5-86, 99-106]
Memoirs [written manuscript in Yiddish, 399 pp., with some clippings and photographs pasted in]

Articles and correspondence [article containing reminiscences of Celia Shapiro; copies of letters from Russian relatives to Boris Shapiro; written translation of B. E. Makovski story by Boris Shapiro], 1943, 1956
"Binder #2" [photocopied articles and pages of a book about Celia Shapiro, describing her arrest and subsequent imprisonment by Russian authorities in 1902, and two letters]
Collection of photocopied articles about Celia Shapiro inspired by a letter written by her while in a Russian prison in 1904

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Revolutsyonnaya Rossiya Nos. 46-48, 50, 1904, Russian-language magazine published by the Socialist Revolutionary Party, one issue containing a letter written by Shapiro; Yiddish-language magazine Der Wecker Vol. XLVI No.1085, 1967; and several typed pages of Shapiro's memoirs, numbered as pp. 484-495

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The publications series consists of issues of various Soviet emigre publications published in Paris, Tel Aviv, and New York, and a Russian-French grammar. There are also some publications in Series III, folder 36.

did.unittitle: Biuleten (Tel Aviv: Nos. 136-139, 1967) [primarily in Russian, occasionally Hebrew and English
Na Rubezhe (Paris: Nos. 1-5, 1951-1952) [Russian language, two copies]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series consists of photographs of Celia Kamenetzky Shapiro, her brother Berel Kamenetzky, Boris Shapiro, Social Revolutionary Yegor Lazarev, and a Mr. and Mrs. Bromberg. There are also a few photographs in Boris Shapiro's memoirs (folders 28-31).
